title: Malrotation with Midgut Volvulus
chief_complaint: My newborn is vomiting green fluid, her belly is distended, and she's very irritable. She was fine until 6 hours ago. Now she's lethargic.

physical_exam.General: Lethargic, hypotonic, poor perfusion. Grey, mottled skin. Capillary refill 5 seconds.
physical_exam.Abdomen: Distended but soft (not rigid - important). Diffuse tenderness. Absent bowel sounds. No peritoneal signs (yet).
physical_exam.Rectal: Empty vault. No meconium in 24 hours.
physical_exam.Hematochezia: Bloody stool (currant jelly stool) passed 1 hour ago - ominous sign of intestinal ischemia.

hidden_data.abdominal_xr: Dilated stomach and proximal duodenum (double bubble sign). Paucity of gas distally. No free air.
hidden_data.upper_gi: Contrast stops at 3rd portion of duodenum with corkscrew appearance (pathognomonic for midgut volvulus). No contrast passes to jejunum.
hidden_data.doppler_us: SMV to left of SMA (abnormal relationship - normal is SMV to right). Whirlpool sign (twisting of mesenteric vessels).
hidden_data.cmp: Na 130, K+ 6.5, Lactate 12, HCO3 8, pH 7.05 (severe metabolic acidosis - bowel ischemia)

correct_diagnosis: Malrotation with Midgut Volvulus and Intestinal Ischemia - SURGICAL EMERGENCY
key_learning_points: Malrotation = abnormal rotation and fixation of midgut during embryologic development (weeks 4-12). Narrow mesenteric base → midgut prone to twisting around SMA → volvulus → obstruction + vascular compromise → bowel ischemia/necrosis within hours

BILIOUS VOMITING in newborn = malrotation with volvulus until proven otherwise. Other causes: Duodenal atresia (double bubble, no distal gas), Jejunal atresia, Meconium ileus, Hirschsprung disease. Bilious vomiting is NEVER normal in a newborn

Classic presentation: Sudden onset bilious vomiting in previously well newborn + Abdominal distension (soft) + Hematochezia (currant jelly stool - mucosal sloughing from ischemia) + Rapid progression to shock. Lethargy = late sign (bowel necrosis, sepsis)

Diagnosis: Upper GI series (gold standard). Findings: Ligament of Treitz not crossing midline, Duodenal obstruction with corkscrew appearance. Ultrasound: Abnormal SMA/SMV relationship (SMV to left of SMA), Whirlpool sign. Do NOT delay surgery for imaging if high clinical suspicion

Treatment: EMERGENCY LAPAROTOMY. Ladd procedure: 1) Detorse volvulus (counterclockwise - 'turn back the hands of time'). 2) Divide Ladd bands (peritoneal bands from cecum to right lateral abdominal wall crossing duodenum). 3) Broaden mesenteric base. 4) Appendectomy (cecum ends up in LUQ - future appendicitis would be diagnostic challenge). Resect necrotic bowel if present (may result in short bowel syndrome). If all bowel necrotic = lethal

Prognosis: >90% survival if surgery before bowel necrosis. If extensive necrosis: Short bowel syndrome requiring long-term TPN. Mortality 5-10% (sepsis, short bowel complications). All cases of bilious vomiting in newborns must be evaluated emergently
